valid_risks.title: Weak lifecycle governance for critical external solutions
valid_risks.why_valid: The end-of-support exposure emerged after the solution had become operationally important.

valid_risks.title: Fragmented accountability across vendor and implementation partner
valid_risks.why_valid: One party can provide workarounds but not the contractual support or warranty commitments of the other.

invalid_risks.title: Primary cyber breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Security implications may arise from end-of-support technology, but there is no confirmed attack or compromise.

invalid_risks.title: Trade sanctions event
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The scenario involves customs processing, not a sanctions or embargo trigger.

invalid_risks.title: Standalone procurement failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The contract may have been procured earlier, but the active event is ongoing third-party dependency and support erosion.

invalid_risks.title: Pure legal enforceability issue
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Contract rights are relevant, but the immediate risk is dependency on the vendor ecosystem rather than court-enforceable interpretation.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: This is best classified as Third-Party because the organization’s exposure comes from reliance on a vendor-partner ecosystem whose support posture no longer matches the business dependency.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Supply Chain is adjacent because regional hardware availability contributes to the issue, but the core dependency is on third-party technology providers.
Operational is adjacent because customs processing continuity is at stake, but the initiating event is vendor support degradation.
Cyber is adjacent because end-of-support components can raise security concerns, but there is no security event as the primary path.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Models may jump to Cyber after seeing cryptographic library and unsupported technology, even though the fact pattern is about vendor dependency and supportability.

scenario_text: A sovereign-adjacent compute program relies on a layered partner stack: a prime cloud integrator, a specialist cooling-services company, a GPU equipment distributor, and a remote firmware-support provider. A dispute between the integrator and the cooling company leads to site attendance being suspended for new high-density racks at the same time the distributor changes payment terms due to tighter credit conditions. No single contract has been terminated, but each party begins limiting performance against obligations that are interdependent across the full service chain. The data-center expansion remains technically recoverable, yet commissioning dates for regulated workloads are now contingent on external parties who each claim another supplier is responsible for the blocker. Internal stakeholders cannot obtain a single accountable recovery plan or a coherent dependency map covering all parties.

scenario_context.operating_context: High-performance compute build-out with interlocking external partners spanning integration, facilities, equipment distribution, and specialist support.

event_path_summary: An interconnected partner ecosystem for a high-density compute program fragments under commercial stress, leaving no clear accountability for restoring progress.
primary_driver: Multi-party external dependency breakdown with ambiguous accountability across the service chain
main_business_consequence: Delay and uncertainty in commissioning strategic compute capacity because external providers are interdependent and not effectively governed as one chain.

valid_risks.title: Opaque multi-tier dependency governance
valid_risks.why_valid: The organization cannot see or manage how interdependent suppliers collectively affect delivery.

valid_risks.title: Absence of single-point accountability across external service chain
valid_risks.why_valid: Each provider is blaming another, indicating weak orchestrated governance of a multi-party arrangement.

valid_risks.title: Third-party financial and performance contagion across a critical program
valid_risks.why_valid: Credit and dispute issues at one provider are propagating into other parties' performance.

valid_risks.title: Inadequate integrated recovery planning with material vendors
valid_risks.why_valid: There is no coherent externally aligned recovery plan for the overall service chain.

invalid_risks.title: Primary supply shortage event
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Parts and terms matter, but the dominant issue is the unmanaged interaction of multiple third parties, not a simple shortage.

invalid_risks.title: Transformation delivery as primary lens
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Program delay is visible, but the event path is driven by external partner-chain failure rather than internal program execution alone.

invalid_risks.title: Business continuity incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The site is not in crisis response mode; the issue is governance of vendor interdependence during expansion.

invalid_risks.title: Legal contract dispute as primary
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Disputes exist, but rights enforcement is not yet the main event path compared with third-party-chain accountability failure.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The root problem is dependency on a multi-tier external ecosystem with no effective chain-wide governance, which makes Third-Party the best primary lens.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Supply Chain is adjacent because equipment and service availability matter, but the key issue is how third parties interact and fail collectively.
Transformation Delivery is adjacent because milestones slip, yet the path to delay begins with vendor-chain breakdown rather than pure program management weakness.
Legal / Contract is adjacent because disputes contribute, but current exposure is broader than a single contract-rights problem.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Models may anchor on compute expansion and delays and classify this as Transformation Delivery instead of recognizing the multi-party vendor-chain trigger.

scenario_text: A managed-service provider that operates a critical multilingual service desk and field-dispatch layer for several group digital platforms has undergone a private-equity backed restructuring that changed regional leadership, subcontractor mix, and debt levels within six months. Core services are still running and no major cyber incident has occurred, yet governance reviews show deteriorating escalation discipline, delayed investment in tooling, and rising dependency on a smaller pool of subcontractors for specialized language and site-support coverage. The main problem is not a current outage or procurement error, but whether the group's dependence on this provider has become materially riskier than its contract dashboards suggest.

scenario_context.operating_context: Outsourced support model for business-critical digital services and multilingual field coverage

event_path_summary: The provider's ownership, subcontracting, and capability profile have shifted in ways that increase dependency risk even before a visible service failure.
primary_driver: Elevated dependency on a destabilizing outsourced provider ecosystem
main_business_consequence: The organization may face degraded leverage and reduced resilience if the provider's capability and governance continue to weaken.

valid_risks.title: Provider dependency deterioration
valid_risks.why_valid: The core issue is rising exposure to a third party whose operating profile has become weaker.

valid_risks.title: Hidden subcontracting concentration
valid_risks.why_valid: Risk is accumulating in the provider's downstream delivery chain.

valid_risks.title: Outsourcing governance blind spot
valid_risks.why_valid: Contract dashboards are understating the risk posed by provider changes.

invalid_risks.title: Procurement weakness
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Original award choices matter, but the current risk lies in the live vendor relationship and dependency evolution.

invalid_risks.title: Supply chain disruption
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Subcontractor changes are present, yet this is not a physical flow problem.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Tooling and provider access matter, but no cyber compromise has occurred.

invalid_risks.title: Business continuity failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: There is no active disruption yet; the concern is dependency on a deteriorating provider.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The lens is Third-Party because the risk sits in dependence on an external service provider whose risk profile has materially shifted.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Business Continuity is adjacent because service failure could follow, but the event today is provider dependency deterioration.
Operational symptoms are emerging but are rooted in the vendor relationship.
Cyber is a confuser because tooling and access exist, yet there is no compromise event.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Classifying living vendor dependency deterioration as Procurement because the relationship started with a sourcing decision.

scenario_text: A specialist external platform handles model monitoring, performance alerts, and remediation ticketing for several customer-facing AI services. During annual assurance, the group discovered that the provider had materially changed its hosting architecture, introduced a new offshore analytics subcontractor, and consolidated some alerting telemetry across clients to support its product roadmap. No breach has been identified and service remains available, but the enterprise now depends on a third party whose architecture, subcontracting, and control boundaries have changed faster than contractual and risk oversight mechanisms have kept up.

scenario_context.operating_context: External platform dependency for AI operations monitoring and remediation workflows

event_path_summary: The provider materially altered its operating model and control perimeter, changing the enterprise's dependency profile before a concrete outage or breach occurred.
primary_driver: Unmanaged change in a critical third-party service architecture and control boundary
main_business_consequence: The group may face amplified operational, privacy, and service-interruption exposure because third-party changes outpaced oversight.

valid_risks.title: Critical vendor change risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The scenario turns on a third party changing materially without the buyer re-underwriting the dependency.

valid_risks.title: Subprocessor and architecture dependency exposure
valid_risks.why_valid: The enterprise now relies on a broader and less transparent provider operating model.

valid_risks.title: Third-party control-boundary drift
valid_risks.why_valid: Risk is created by how the external provider changed its environment and governance.

invalid_risks.title: Cyber incident
invalid_risks.why_invalid: A breach is possible later, but no compromise triggered the scenario.

invalid_risks.title: Business continuity breakdown
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Services remain running; the issue is rising dependency risk, not active outage.

invalid_risks.title: Procurement design flaw

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The primary lens is Third-Party because the event is about dependency on an external provider whose risk profile changed materially.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Cyber is adjacent because control changes could increase attack exposure, but no compromise occurred.
Business Continuity is nearby because the provider is critical, yet there is no disruption event.
Data Governance / Privacy is secondary because telemetry use changed, but provider dependency is the central theme.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Misclassifying control-boundary drift at a vendor as Cyber or Privacy before any actual breach occurs.

scenario_text: A finance and procurement transformation programme introduced a new group-wide approval workflow intended to standardize spend control across corporate functions and technology business units. The configuration went live on time, but multiple process steps were mapped from legacy policies rather than the newly approved target operating model. As a result, users can complete transactions, yet the programme is delivering duplicated approvals and manual rework instead of the simplified process promised in the business case.

scenario_context.operating_context: Enterprise process redesign and system rollout across support functions

event_path_summary: A programme delivered its technology milestone but failed to realize the intended target-state process design and benefits.
primary_driver: Poor translation of target operating model into programme design and configuration
main_business_consequence: Transformation benefits are diluted because the new workflow preserves complexity and rework.

valid_risks.title: Benefit realization failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The programme delivered go-live but not the intended simplification outcomes.

valid_risks.title: Design-to-build translation risk
valid_risks.why_valid: The core issue is that approved target-state design was not properly embedded in configuration.

valid_risks.title: Transformation outcome drift
valid_risks.why_valid: The delivered solution has drifted away from the stated transformation objectives.

invalid_risks.title: Sanctions screening breach
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No restricted-party or sanctions event appears.

invalid_risks.title: Physical intrusion at a facility
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No site security event caused the issue.

invalid_risks.title: Supplier bankruptcy
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No external supplier distress drives the scenario.

invalid_risks.title: Fraudulent reimbursement claim
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No misconduct or false claim is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The root issue is programme delivery against a target-state design and expected benefits, which is Transformation Delivery.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational symptoms appear after go-live, but they result from a programme delivery gap.
Procurement is adjacent because the process covers spend approvals, yet the failure is not a sourcing event.
Financial value is affected through missed savings, but that is a consequence of poor transformation delivery.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Treating post-go-live process pain as merely Operational and missing the programme benefit-realization failure.

scenario_text: A multi-entity data-platform consolidation programme is migrating analytics workloads from several legacy environments into a shared lakehouse architecture. Steering committees report green status because migration milestones are being hit, but business units keep postponing decommissioning of old platforms since reconciliation tolerances, reporting ownership, and service-support boundaries were never fully agreed. The programme is on track technically, yet the transformation case assumed legacy exit and operating-model simplification within the same fiscal year.

scenario_context.operating_context: Enterprise platform consolidation across analytics, data engineering, and reporting functions

event_path_summary: The programme is meeting technical milestones but failing to deliver operating-model transition and legacy-exit outcomes needed for value realization.
primary_driver: Incomplete business design and transition management within a transformation programme
main_business_consequence: The enterprise carries duplicated cost and complexity because legacy environments cannot be retired as planned.

valid_risks.title: Transformation value leakage
valid_risks.why_valid: Technical delivery without legacy exit undermines the economic and operating benefits of the programme.

valid_risks.title: Business transition planning failure
valid_risks.why_valid: Critical decisions on ownership and support boundaries were not resolved in time.

valid_risks.title: Milestone-versus-outcome misalignment
valid_risks.why_valid: Status reporting is focused on technical progress rather than delivery of intended business outcomes.

invalid_risks.title: Warehouse theft
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No physical asset theft or security breach is involved.

invalid_risks.title: Customs detention of equipment
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No border movement issue drives the scenario.

invalid_risks.title: Conflict-of-interest in vendor selection

invalid_risks.title: Emergency evacuation failure
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No incident requiring life-safety response appears.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: This is a classic programme-delivery issue where milestones look healthy but transformation outcomes and benefits are not landing.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational pain exists because legacy systems remain, but that is downstream of programme design and transition management.
Data Governance / Privacy is adjacent because retention and reporting boundaries matter, yet governance is not the root event path.
Strategic considerations exist around platform simplification, but the immediate failure is in programme delivery execution.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Calling this Data Governance / Privacy because data topics are visible, even though the core problem is transformation execution.

scenario_text: An enterprise workforce transformation is introducing a new field-service scheduling model for industrial inspection, digital infrastructure maintenance, and specialist lab support. The design assumed one common set of role definitions and productivity assumptions across all business units, but pilot results show that travel patterns, certification requirements, standby coverage, and customer-site access rules vary far more than expected. Programme leaders are still pushing the original rollout timeline to preserve reported savings targets.

scenario_context.operating_context: Cross-functional operating-model redesign spanning technical field services and specialist support teams

event_path_summary: A transformation programme is forcing a standardized target model that pilot evidence shows does not fit real operating conditions across business units.
primary_driver: Programme design and rollout decisions are ignoring operational fit evidence
main_business_consequence: The transformation risks failed adoption, service disruption, and missed benefits because the target model is not implementable as designed.

valid_risks.title: Target operating model fit failure
valid_risks.why_valid: Pilot evidence shows the designed future state does not match actual business requirements.

valid_risks.title: Rollout decision bias risk
valid_risks.why_valid: Leadership is pressing ahead to protect timelines and savings despite contrary evidence.

valid_risks.title: Transformation adoption risk
valid_risks.why_valid: If the model does not fit operations, adoption and sustained benefit delivery will fail.

invalid_risks.title: Intentional falsification of time sheets
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No misconduct or fraud is described; the issue is programme design.

why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: People / Workforce is adjacent because roles and scheduling are affected, but the issue arises from programme design choices.
Operational consequences may follow, yet the causal driver is within the transformation effort.
Strategic ambition for standardization exists, but the present problem is delivery against reality, not strategy formulation.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Reclassifying this as People / Workforce because staffing topics dominate the narrative.

scenario_text: A group-wide enterprise resource planning and contract-lifecycle modernization programme spans procurement, legal, finance, and managed-service operations across the UAE, Europe, and South Asia. Workstreams are individually progressing, but cutover sequencing assumes that master-data harmonization, delegated-authority redesign, supplier segmentation, and contract-template rationalization will all mature at the same pace. In reality, legal clause libraries remain unresolved for several jurisdictions, supplier ownership data is incomplete, and regional finance teams still use different accrual logic. Programme reporting emphasizes schedule confidence, yet the integrated dependency map is weak and decision rights across workstreams remain blurred.

scenario_context.operating_context: Complex multi-workstream modernization programme affecting core control and service processes

event_path_summary: A large programme faces elevated failure risk because interdependent workstreams are not being integrated or governed as one delivery system.
primary_driver: Weak dependency management and cross-workstream governance in a major transformation
main_business_consequence: The modernization effort risks unstable cutover, delayed benefits, and prolonged dual running because integrated readiness is overstated.

valid_risks.title: Programme integration failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The central issue is weak management of dependencies across tightly linked transformation workstreams.

valid_risks.title: Governance-driven delivery risk
valid_risks.why_valid: Blurred decision rights and optimistic reporting are undermining reliable execution.

valid_risks.title: Integrated readiness overstatement
valid_risks.why_valid: Workstreams report progress individually while the combined readiness picture is materially weaker.

invalid_risks.title: DDoS attack on ERP
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No malicious cyber disruption caused the delivery risk.

invalid_risks.why_invalid: No integrity misconduct drives the event path.

invalid_risks.title: Import tariff increase

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The scenario centers on how a complex programme is governed and integrated, not on any one legal, financial, or operational topic within it.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational impacts will follow from a poor cutover, but the originating lens is programme delivery governance.
Legal / Contract matters because clause libraries are unresolved, yet that is one dependency among many.
Financial topics appear through accrual logic and controls, but they do not explain the broader delivery failure.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Selecting the most domain-specific dependency mentioned, such as Legal / Contract or Financial, instead of recognizing programme integration failure.

scenario_text: A group-wide modernization program spanning ERP, contract lifecycle management, supplier onboarding, and shared analytics has maintained green milestone reporting by tracking each workstream independently. An integrated readiness review, however, found that master data, policy redesign, decision rights, and regional process cutovers are not maturing at the same pace, and several apparent milestones are only locally true because teams adopted different assumptions about the target operating model. Strategy, operating risk, and partner dependencies are all plausible confusers, but the dominant issue is that the transformation is being governed as parallel projects rather than one interdependent delivery system.

scenario_context.operating_context: Complex enterprise modernization affecting finance, legal, procurement, and operations across regions

event_path_summary: Independent milestone reporting masked the fact that tightly coupled workstreams are not converging into integrated delivery readiness.
primary_driver: Program integration and dependency-management failure
main_business_consequence: The enterprise faces unstable cutover, delayed benefits, and prolonged dual running because integrated readiness is overstated.

valid_risks.title: Interdependent program failure
valid_risks.why_valid: The key issue is inability to manage the modernization as one integrated transformation.

valid_risks.title: Dependency-management weakness
valid_risks.why_valid: Cross-workstream dependencies are not being governed tightly enough.

valid_risks.title: Outcome-versus-milestone misalignment
valid_risks.why_valid: Reported progress does not reflect whether the transformation can land safely as a whole.

invalid_risks.title: Strategic issue
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The target direction may be valid; the problem is delivery integration rather than strategic choice.

invalid_risks.title: Operational problem
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Operations will be affected, but the main issue is transformation execution.

invalid_risks.title: Third-party dependency
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Partners may contribute, yet no vendor failure explains the integration gap.

invalid_risks.why_invalid: No ownership structure or investment vehicle is at the center.

why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Strategic is adjacent because the transformation supports enterprise direction, but the active issue is execution of the program.
Operational is plausible because cutover and dual running affect operations, yet those are downstream of delivery weakness.
Third-Party is secondary because external implementers may be involved, not because they are the root cause.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Calling multi-workstream integration problems Strategic because the program is enterprise-wide.

scenario_text: A digital operating-model transformation intended to unify service management across cloud, data, and industrial-edge support functions was built around a standard process blueprint and a new orchestration toolset. Pilots succeeded in controlled environments, but as rollout expanded, exceptions for local regulation, language, site access, and customer-specific escalation commitments multiplied faster than the design authority could absorb. The result is not merely operational messiness; it is a transformation that is now delivering a target state no one can clearly define, with benefits, accountabilities, and technology configuration drifting apart across regions.

scenario_context.operating_context: Rollout of a common service-management target model across diverse digital and industrial support functions

event_path_summary: As rollout expanded, the designed future state fragmented under unmanaged local exceptions, undermining the transformation itself.
primary_driver: Target-state design drift and rollout control failure
main_business_consequence: The enterprise may fail to realize promised benefits because the transformation is no longer delivering one coherent operating model.

valid_risks.title: Target-state fragmentation
valid_risks.why_valid: The main problem is that the future-state design is no longer coherent as rollout expands.

valid_risks.title: Rollout-control failure
valid_risks.why_valid: Local exceptions are overrunning the ability of the program to maintain one design.

valid_risks.title: Benefit-realization breakdown in transformation
valid_risks.why_valid: Technology and process rollout are diverging from intended outcomes.

invalid_risks.title: Operational issue
invalid_risks.why_invalid: Operational inconsistency is visible, but it is a symptom of transformation failure.

invalid_risks.title: Strategic problem
invalid_risks.why_invalid: The direction may still be sound; the issue is the inability to implement it coherently.

invalid_risks.title: Third-party issue
invalid_risks.why_invalid: External commitments and partners add complexity, yet they do not define the primary event.

invalid_risks.title: Investment / JV angle
invalid_risks.why_invalid: No investment vehicle or JV structure is involved.

evaluation_notes.why_primary_lens_is_correct: The primary lens is Transformation Delivery because the future-state design and rollout governance have broken down.
why_nearby_lenses_are_not_primary: Operational is adjacent because service inconsistency is visible in live teams, but the origin is the failing transformation program.
Strategic is plausible because enterprise design choices matter, yet the main issue is delivering the agreed target state.
Third-Party is secondary because customer and partner obligations complicate rollout, not because vendor dependency caused it.
evaluation_notes.common_model_failure_mode: Confusing transformation design drift with ordinary Operational inconsistency.
